$comment: OB-5 — the authored Epoch-0 for an EAST_COAST/origins founding. The first stage of the story: a fully-written birth → the doctor's date → growing up in a Gilded-Age industrial port (class/station emerging from a city with a hard wall between old money and the immigrant tide), feeding into the shared gender → naming → calling → partner chain. Each choice sets a CAUSAL ANCHOR the butterfly/world engine reads. Place-gated to east_coast; higher weight than the generic beats so the East Coast line opens on its own prose. Culture: anglo_protestant. Cue: coal smoke, harbor brine, port-city clamor. Trope: oligarch (the industrial-capital ladder).

events.title: Born in the Smoke
events.scene: You come into the world in a narrow brick row-house wedged between a tannery and the harbor, where the coal smoke never fully clears and the foghorns mark the hours better than any clock. Below the window, the port never sleeps — cranes, drays, the brine-and-tar reek of a city that runs on what comes off the ships. A doctor sent up by the parish charity, harried and competent, finishes his work and opens the small book where he records the city's newest arrivals. You hear him read the date as he writes it down.
events.researchNote: OB-5 birth opener for the East Coast. Sets `emerged` so the Epoch-0 chain begins; narrates the seed-drawn date via {birth_date}. Gilded-Age industrial port — coal smoke, harbor, the immigrant tenement city, 1885.

choices.text: You overhear the date he says — the year that fixes when you are.
choices.outcome: “{birth_date},” the doctor murmurs, and closes the book on one more name. In this city a person is born owing — to the landlord, the boss, the machine — and the only question that ever matters is whether the line will one day be owed TO.

events.title: A Name in the New World
events.scene: The {family_name} name is fixed — but the given name is the family's first quiet wager on which America the child will belong to. Some anglicize hard, reaching for the names the old families on the hill carry. Some hold the old country close in defiance. Some pick a plain, sturdy name that asks nothing and gives nothing away. The choice is made; you become {full_name}.
events.researchNote: OB-5: given-naming as an immigrant family's first strategic bet on assimilation vs. defiance in a stratified port city. Given name dealt from the anglo_protestant pool at founding; this beat sets `named` and frames how it was chosen — an early personality anchor distinct from Ireland's parish framing and the West's open reinvention.

choices.text: Named to climb — a name that could pass on the hill where the old money lives.
choices.outcome: A name chosen like a key cut for a lock the family does not yet own. Ambition starts at the font.

choices.text: Named in defiance — the old country worn openly, whatever the hill thinks.
choices.outcome: A name that will not pretend to be something it isn't. The neighborhood approves; the climb just got steeper, and prouder.

choices.text: A plain, sturdy name — nothing to live up to, nothing to apologize for.
choices.outcome: No hill, no old country — just a working name for a working city. It will wear well wherever the line ends up.

events.title: What the City Teaches
events.scene: You are old enough now to read the city, and the city is a wall. There is the hill, where the old families live behind iron fences and never once look down at the harbor that pays for them. There is the ward, where everyone you know lives, where the boss's machine trades coal and jobs and a Christmas turkey for a vote. And there is the factory, which eats twelve hours of a day and hands back just enough to do it again tomorrow. You watch where the doors are — and, more carefully, where the doors are NOT.
events.researchNote: OB-5: class/station emerges early as an organic response to observing the form + function of Gilded-Age port life (user). A hard, stratified ladder (old money / political machine / factory), versus the West's open mobility or Ireland's rural tenancy. Sets an early-pressure anchor the calling reads.

choices.text: You look up at the hill and decide you will live behind those fences one day.
choices.outcome: Some children learn to keep their eyes level. You keep yours up the hill, and the wanting will not leave you now.

choices.text: You study the boss's machine — how a vote becomes a job becomes power.
choices.outcome: You see that the hill has the money but the ward has the votes, and the man who counts the votes can make the hill come to HIM. You mean to be that man.

choices.text: You trust the church and the neighborhood, like the steadiest of the ward.
choices.outcome: There is a strength in a community that holds together against the wall. Whether solidarity alone can found a line is a question for later years.

events.title: The Parish School and the Priest Who Saw You
events.scene: There is a crowded parish school and a teacher — Father Doyle, who runs the boys like a press gang and quotes Latin and the prizefights in the same breath. He has sent a few of his pupils up the hill and watched the city break more of them. He stops you after the bell one day and looks at you the way a foreman looks at good ore. "You've a future in you, boy," he says. "The trouble with this city is it'll spend your future for you if you don't spend it first."
events.researchNote: OB-5: the calling EMERGES (user) — not one static pick. A crowded parish school + a shrewd, fight-quoting priest is the first pressure that bends the child toward a bent. Sets `schooled`, an anchor the crystallization reads. Distinct in tone from Ireland's embittered Mr. Lynch and the West's drifter Miss Reyes — Father Doyle is an operator inside the machine city.

choices.text: You read past every book the school owns — hungry for the wider world.
choices.outcome: Father Doyle starts smuggling you books from the rectory. A door has opened that the ward cannot quite close behind you.

choices.text: You learn the boys and the bosses both — you read people, not primers.
choices.outcome: You discover you can turn a room — or a ward — with a handshake and the right word. In a machine city, that gift is currency.

choices.text: You leave school for the factory floor — the family needs the wage now.
choices.outcome: Father Doyle blesses you and lets you go to the whistle. The floor does not care what you might have been — only how many hours you can stand at the machine.

events.title: What You Are For
events.scene: You are a young {given_name} {surname} now, grown into the body and the hard, climbing city that made you. The vague pulls of the ward have set into a direction. In this city a person is either climbing or being climbed over — there is no standing still on the wall. A man has to plant a flag the family can rally to for a hundred years. Standing at the edge of your own life, with the hill above you and the harbor below, you feel which way you will go.
events.researchNote: OB-5: the calling CRYSTALLIZES into the line's archetype (user: calling IS the archetype, and it EMERGES). All six archetypes offered; setsArchetype commits the power base. Prior schooling/station anchors color which feel natural. Self-excludes the generic ev_birth_calling via the OB-5 has_authored_epoch0 flag.

choices.text: Money. You will own the factory, the dock, and the company the hill keeps its fortune in.
choices.outcome: The Magnate's road: in this city the wall is made of money, so you will become the wall. The line will own the works the whole port reports to — and the hill will learn your name whether it likes it or not.

choices.text: Power. You will run the machine, and the machine runs the city.
choices.outcome: The Statesman's road: the ward, the bosses, the patient ledger of favors owed. The line will count votes the way the hill counts dollars — and find that, in a city, votes win.

choices.text: Faith. You will be the rock the whole ward leans on when the city is cruel.
choices.outcome: The Prophet's road: the parish, the pulpit, the charity that catches what the factory throws away. The line will hold souls — and a congregation, in a city like this, is a power the bosses fear.

choices.text: Invention. You will build the engines and the steel the new century will run on.
choices.outcome: The Visionary's road: the works, the patent, the machine that does the labor of a hundred men. The line will make what the age cannot yet imagine — and sell it to everyone who must.

choices.text: The crowd. You have the gift the vaudeville houses and the new screens are starved for.
choices.outcome: The Star's road: the stage, the marquee, the packed house that does not care which side of the wall you came from. The line will be adored across the whole city — and adoration, here, opens doors that money cannot.

choices.text: The body. You are the hardest, fastest thing the ward ever put up, and the crowds will roar.
choices.outcome: The Champion's road: the prize ring, the ballpark, the contest a whole port turns out to bet on. The line will win its standing with its own fists and feet — earned in front of the city, owed to no boss.
